title: शंकराचार्य अष्टकम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री शंकराचार्य अष्टकम् ॥

श्रुतिस्मृतिपुराणानामालयं करुणालयम्।
नमामि भगवत्पादशंकरं लोकशंकरम्॥ १॥

शंकरं शंकराचार्यं केशवं बादरायणम्।
सूत्रभाष्यकृतौ वन्दे भगवन्तौ पुनः पुनः॥ २॥

अष्टवर्षे चतुर्वेदी द्वादशे सर्वशास्त्रविद्।
षोडशे कृतवान्भाष्यं द्वात्रिंशे मुनिरभ्यगात्॥ ३॥

मण्डनमिश्रं जित्वा च सुरेश्वरं कृत्वा शिष्यम्।
चतुर्धामस्थापनकृत्शंकरं प्रणमामि तम्॥ ४॥

ब्रह्मसूत्रभाष्यकृतं विवेकचूडामणिप्रदम्।
आत्मबोधप्रकाशिनं शंकरं प्रणमामि तम्॥ ५॥

भजगोविन्दं गोविन्दं मूढमते सम्प्राप्ते सन्निहिते काले।
नहि नहि रक्षति डुकृंकरणे शंकरं प्रणमामि तम्॥ ६॥

केरलजन्मधरं देवं कालटीग्रामसम्भवम्।
आर्यम्बासुतमीशानं शंकरं प्रणमामि तम्॥ ७॥

अद्वैतवेदान्तस्थापनकर्तारं जगद्गुरुम्।
चतुराम्नायपीठेशं शंकरं प्रणमामि तम्॥ ८॥

॥ इति श्री शंकराचार्य अष्टकम् सम्पूर्णम् ॥
benefits: Sacred Shankaracharya Ashtakam for devotion and spiritual growth
Invokes blessings of Shankaracharya in all forms
Traditionally recited for removal of sins and bestows spiritual merit
Ideal for Shankaracharya festivals and daily worship
description: Among the most cherished prayers to Lord Shiva, Shankaracharya Ashtakam (शंकराचार्य अष्टकम्) stands as an eight-verse devotional hymn. Attributed to Advaita Tradition, this prayer holds a special place in Hindu devotional literature. Regular recitation is believed to grant divine protection and auspiciousness, invoke blessings of Shankaracharya in all forms, and remove sins and bestow spiritual merit. It is especially recommended on Thursday (Guruvar) and Monday (Somvar) and during Guru Purnima and Shankaracharya Jayanti for maximum spiritual benefit. Comprising 8 verses, it can be completed in approximately 4 minutes, making it suitable for daily devotional practice. On this page, you can read the complete Shankaracharya Ashtakam in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Shankaracharya Ashtakam in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning. Sacred hymn for Shankaracharya devotion.
relatedSearches: Adi Shankara four mathas Sringeri Puri Dwarka Jyotirmath
Bhaja Govindam Mudha Mate Adi Shankara
Mandana Mishra debate with Adi Shankara story

seoFaqs.question: Who is being praised in the Shankaracharya Ashtakam?
seoFaqs.answer: The Ashtakam praises Adi Shankara Bhagavatpada (788 to 820 CE per traditional dating), the Kerala-born philosopher-saint who reorganised Sanatana Dharma, established the four cardinal mathas, and authored the Brahma-sutra-bhashya, Upanishad-bhashyas, Bhagavad-gita-bhashya and devotional hymns including Bhaja Govindam and Saundarya Lahari. The hymn is sometimes also recited for the four current Shankaracharyas heading the Sringeri, Puri, Dwarka and Jyotirmath mathas, who carry his title and parampara. Verse 6 directly quotes the famous Bhaja Govindam line, identifying the original Adi Shankara as the focus.

seoFaqs.question: What is the source of the Shankaracharya Ashtakam?
seoFaqs.answer: The Shankaracharya Ashtakam belongs to the post-Shankara Advaita-tradition stotra corpus and is preserved in Smarta and Dashanami sannyasi liturgical compendia. Several variant Ashtakams praising Adi Shankara exist; the present text follows the widely-recited eight-verse form that summarises his birthplace at Kaladi, his Kerala origin from mother Aryamba, his composition of the Brahma-sutra-bhashya, his establishment of the four amnaya-pithas, and his role as Jagadguru of Advaita Vedanta. It is recited especially on Shankara Jayanti (Vaishakha Shukla Panchami) and Guru Purnima.

seoFaqs.question: What are the four amnaya pithas referenced in the Ashtakam?
seoFaqs.answer: The eighth verse identifies Adi Shankara as Chatur-amnaya-pithesha, the lord of the four amnaya pithas. These are the four cardinal monastic seats Shankara established to preserve and propagate Advaita Vedanta: Sringeri Sharada Pitham in Karnataka (south, associated with the Yajurveda), Govardhana Pitham at Puri in Odisha (east, Rigveda), Dwarka Sharada Pitham in Gujarat (west, Sama Veda) and Jyotir Math at Joshimath in Uttarakhand (north, Atharva Veda). Each pitha is headed by a Shankaracharya in unbroken parampara from Adi Shankara himself, and each is associated with one of his four principal disciples: Sureshwara, Padmapada, Hastamalaka and Totakacharya.

seoFaqs.question: What is the significance of the Bhaja Govindam line in verse 6?
seoFaqs.answer: Verse 6 quotes the opening of Adi Shankara's celebrated Bhaja Govindam (Mohamudgara), which warns that when the time of death approaches no amount of grammatical knowledge of the Sanskrit verbs (dukrin-karane) will save the seeker; only worship of Govinda will. Tradition narrates that Shankara composed this on seeing an old Sanskrit grammarian rote-memorising Panini's verb-roots in Varanasi. The line summarises Shankara's view that bhakti to Govinda is the practical fruit of the Advaita-jnana he taught, refuting any caricature of Advaita as dry intellectualism. Quoting it within the Ashtakam roots Shankara's philosophical authority in his own devotional voice.

seoFaqs.question: When should the Shankaracharya Ashtakam be recited?
seoFaqs.answer: Shankara Jayanti, falling on Vaishakha Shukla Panchami (April or May), is the primary day. Guru Purnima in Ashadha is the second-most prescribed occasion since Adi Shankara is regarded as the Jagadguru, the world-teacher. Thursday (Guruvar) is the weekly day for guru-bhakti and Monday (Somvar) honours his identification with Shiva (Shankara is a name of Shiva and tradition holds Adi Shankara to be a Shiva amsha). Daily recitation by sadhakas of Advaita Vedanta is also common. There are no caste or gender restrictions; the hymn is part of the open Smarta liturgy.

seoFaqs.question: What benefits does the Shankaracharya Ashtakam confer?
seoFaqs.answer: Recitation is held to grant guru-kripa, clarity in the study of Vedanta, removal of doubts about non-dual realisation, blessings of all four amnaya-pithas at once, eligibility for sannyasa or monastic discipline for those so inclined, and ultimately the same goal Adi Shankara himself articulated: jivanmukti, liberation while living through direct knowledge of Brahman. Many Smarta households recite it before beginning study of any of Shankara's bhashyas as a preliminary guru-vandana.
